g.data-narration: Welcome to One-Way ANOVA, a statistical technique to test differences between group means using a single factor.
g.text: Analysis of Variance - Testing Means Across Multiple Groups

g.data-narration: One-Way ANOVA extends the t-test to compare means of three or more groups based on one independent variable.
g.text: A statistical technique to test whether there are significant differences
between the means of two or more independent groups.

g.data-narration: The null hypothesis states all group means are equal, while the alternative states at least one mean differs.
g.text: Not all population means are equal

g.data-narration: ANOVA requires three key assumptions: independence of observations, normality of distributions, and equal variances.
Key notation: k groups, n observations per group, N total, with x̄ᵢ as group means and x̄ as grand mean.

g.text: xᵢⱼ = jᵗʰ observation in group i

g.data-narration: Total Sum of Squares (SST) measures overall variability by summing squared deviations from the grand mean.
g.text: Measures total variability in all data

g.data-narration: Between-Groups Sum of Squares (SSB) measures variability due to differences between group means and grand mean.
g.text: Variability due to differences between group means

g.data-narration: Within-Groups Sum of Squares (SSW) measures variability within each group around their respective means.
The fundamental ANOVA identity shows total variation equals between-groups plus within-groups variation.
Degrees of freedom partition: total is N-1, between is k-1, and within is N-k, summing correctly.
Mean squares are sum of squares divided by their degrees of freedom, representing variance estimates.
The F-statistic follows an F-distribution; if calculated F exceeds critical F, we reject the null hypothesis.
The ANOVA table organizes all components: Source, Sum of Squares, df, Mean Square, and F-statistic.
In this example, three teaching methods show significantly different scores with F=34.48 far exceeding F-critical=3.89.
g.text: Comparing test scores across 3 methods (n=5 each, N=15, k=3)
Conclusion: Significant difference exists between teaching methods

g.data-narration: Example 2 tests four fertilizers showing F=6.22 exceeding F-critical=4.07, indicating significant yield differences.
Example 3 shows battery brands with F=0.80 less than F-critical=4.26, failing to reject equal means.
g.text: Worked Example 3: Battery Brands (Fail to Reject)
Three brands (k=3, n=4 each, N=12) - Similar means: 49.5, 50.5, 49.5

g.data-narration: Master these essential ANOVA formulas: SST identity, mean squares, F-statistic, and grand mean calculation.
For exams: state hypotheses clearly, show complete ANOVA table, compare F-values, and always conclude properly.
g.text: One-Way ANOVA: Your Foundation for Advanced Experimental Design
